name: queries
context: Write, execute, and optimize PostgreSQL queries: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, CTEs, window functions
info: This skill covers the full range of PostgreSQL query operations: basic CRUD with SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE; Common Table Expressions (CTEs) for breaking complex queries into readable steps; window functions for analytics, ranking, and running totals; parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection; and transaction management with BEGIN, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK. The pg (node-postgres) library is the primary driver, using parameterized queries with $1, $2 placeholders. All queries should use parameterized inputs to prevent injection. CTEs and window functions are PostgreSQL strengths that enable complex analytics without application-level processing.

sections.title: Basic CRUD
sections.body: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE with the pg driver. Always use `$1, $2` placeholders for parameterized queries. Use `RETURNING` on INSERT/UPDATE to get back the affected rows without a separate SELECT. Use `ON CONFLICT` for upserts. See [[postgres/performance#explain-analyze]] for optimizing slow queries.

sections.title: Common Table Expressions
sections.body: CTEs use `WITH name AS (...)` syntax to define named subqueries. Chain multiple CTEs with commas. Recursive CTEs use `WITH RECURSIVE` for tree traversal and hierarchical data. CTEs improve readability and allow reuse of intermediate results. PostgreSQL materializes CTEs by default in older versions; in v12+ use `NOT MATERIALIZED` hint if the planner should inline them. See [[postgres/performance#explain-analyze]] to verify CTE execution plans.

sections.title: Window functions
sections.body: Window functions compute across a set of rows related to the current row. Key functions: `ROW_NUMBER()`, `RANK()`, `DENSE_RANK()`, `NTILE()`, `LAG()`, `LEAD()`, `SUM() OVER()`, `AVG() OVER()`. Use `PARTITION BY` to group and `ORDER BY` to sequence within partitions. Frame clauses (`ROWS BETWEEN ...`) control the window bounds. Window functions avoid the need for self-joins and subqueries in analytics.

sections.title: Parameterized queries
sections.body: Always use `$1, $2, ...` placeholders with the pg driver. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection and enable query plan caching via prepared statements. For dynamic column or table names (which cannot be parameterized), use a whitelist of allowed names and validate strictly.

sections.title: Transactions
sections.body: Use `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK` for multi-statement atomicity. Acquire a dedicated client from the pool with `pool.connect()`. Always release the client in a `finally` block. Use `SAVEPOINT` for nested transaction checkpoints. Set isolation levels with `SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL` (READ COMMITTED default, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE). See [[postgres/performance#connection-pooling]] for pool sizing with transactions.
